_note: Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987, Section 12 — who is entitled to FREE legal aid. This is the headline feature (arch doc §0.3, §Legal-domain). Under s.13, entitlement needs only a prima-facie case and a simple income affidavit is generally sufficient, so the friction to claim aid is low. The Escalation/Aid agent scans the citizen's own words for these cues; when one matches, it can say with confidence 'you likely qualify'. This is a lookup, not a generation — the biggest, safest, highest-impact output the box produces.

categories.label: a woman
categories.explanation: All women are entitled to free legal aid regardless of income.

categories.label: a child (under 18)
categories.explanation: Children under 18 are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: a member of a Scheduled Caste / Scheduled Tribe
categories.explanation: Members of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: a person with a disability
categories.explanation: Persons with disabilities are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: an industrial workman / worker
categories.explanation: Industrial workmen — factory, construction and industrial workers — are entitled to free legal aid. This covers most unpaid-wage cases.

categories.label: a victim of trafficking or forced/bonded labour
categories.explanation: Victims of trafficking or forced labour (begar) under Article 23 are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: a person in custody
categories.explanation: Persons in custody (including protective, juvenile and psychiatric facilities) are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: a victim of a mass disaster / atrocity
categories.explanation: Victims of mass disasters — floods, earthquakes, drought, caste atrocities, ethnic or communal violence — are entitled to free legal aid.

categories.label: a person below the state income ceiling
categories.explanation: Anyone below the state-set income ceiling qualifies (commonly Rs 1-3 lakh/year for lower courts; the exact figure is state-specific and must be verified). A simple income affidavit is generally enough.
